title: Resonant optical cooling of nuclear spins in case of strong Knight field of photoexcited electrons
abstract: Resonant cooling of the nuclear spin system of a semiconductor by spin-polarized charge carriers under pumping with helicity-modulated polarized light is considered theoretically. It is shown that in the case of strong Knight field of charge carriers, exceeding local fields of the dipole-dipole interaction of nuclear spins, the Overhauser field arising as a result of resonant cooling can considerably modify the overall shape of magnetic-field dependences of charge carrier spin polarization, experimentally observed as the Hanle effect.
summary: This paper theoretically explores how a strong, oscillating Knight field from photoexcited electrons can resonantly cool nuclear spins in semiconductors. It demonstrates that this cooling process creates an Overhauser field that alters the Hanle effect, providing a way to identify the sign of the carrier g-factor.
main_problem: The paper investigates the theoretical description of resonant cooling of nuclear spins in semiconductors when the Knight field of photoexcited electrons is strong enough to exceed local nuclear dipole-dipole interaction fields.
main_result: It is shown that the resulting Overhauser field can significantly modify the shape of Hanle effect curves, and the specific shape of these curves can be used to determine the sign of the charge carrier g-factor.
method: A modified rotating frame method is used to decompose the oscillating Knight field into two components, followed by solving a nonlinear balance equation between dynamic nuclear polarization and spin-lattice relaxation.
model_or_system: The system consists of a semiconductor nuclear spin system interacting with spin-polarized charge carriers under illumination with helicity-modulated (alternating circular polarization) light.
key_observables: Hanle curves (magnetic depolarization of photoluminescence), Overhauser field (BN), and electron spin polarization.
important_parameters: External magnetic field (B), Knight field amplitude (Be) and frequency (omega), nuclear spin-lattice relaxation time (T1), and charge carrier g-factor.
main_assumptions: The frequency of the Knight field modulation is assumed to be far from the frequency range of the nuclear spin correlator, and the Knight field is assumed to be large compared to local nuclear fields.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of effective fields in two different rotating frames; Figure 2 displays numerical examples of Hanle curves at different modulation frequencies demonstrating different shapes for positive vs. negative g-factors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of resonant cooling, describes the physical model and the rotating frame method, presents the mathematical framework for calculating DNP rates, provides numerical results for Hanle curves, and concludes with the implications for determining the g-factor.
why_interesting: The paper explores the non-equilibrium dynamics of spin systems under periodic driving and the back-action of a polarized reservoir (nuclear spins) on the driving species (electrons), which is relevant to open quantum systems and driven many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A sketch of effective fields and spin components in the two rotating frames: a) the frame rotating along with the Larmor precession of nuclear spins and b) the frame rotating opposite to the Larmor precession. The generated average spin is projected onto the total field in the rotating frame, and then onto the constant effective field in the laboratory frame.

FIG. 2. Examples of Hanle curves under resonant cooling of nuclear spins for several modulation frequencies correspond- ing to resonance fields Bres for the following parameters of the electron-nuclear spin system: be = 200G, bN T1 T1e = 2000G, S0 = 0.1, B1/2 = 100G. a) negative g-factor of charge carri- ers, b) positive g-factor of charge carriers (the nuclear mag- netic moment is positive for both cases)
